Patch Set 2:

> (1 comment)

If we come to this point in the function, it means our node is a router. If check_routers_before_use is enabled, we do want to consider the peer NI of the router to be alive if we are checking it - so we can ping it to proactively check on it. 
We can probably assume that the message our router initiates to the other router is to check if it is alive. So how about the following:
        
if (!list_empty(&lpni->lpni_peer_net->lpn_peer->lp_routes) &&
    !msg->msg_routing &&
    lpni->lpni_ns_status == LNET_NI_STATUS_DOWN &&
    lnet_peers_start_down())
           return true;

This narrows it down so that non-router nodes are not affected.

Patch Set 2: Verified+1

Passed enforced test review-ldiskfs-dne on CentOS 8.5/x86_64 uploaded by Onyx Autotest 2 from onyx-72vm1: https://testing.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/d545c578-36dc-40f2-a0a9-1e31efd4716a ran 5 tests.

Patch Set 2: Code-Review-1

This change cannot be cherry-picked to master.
Please rebase the change locally and upload the rebased commit for review.

Patch Set 2:

> > (1 comment)
 > 
 > If we come to this point in the function, it means our node is a
 > router. If check_routers_before_use is enabled, we do want to
 > consider the peer NI of the router to be alive if we are checking
 > it - so we can ping it to proactively check on it.
 > We can probably assume that the message our router initiates to the
 > other router is to check if it is alive. So how about the
 > following:
 > 
 > if (!list_empty(&lpni->lpni_peer_net->lpn_peer->lp_routes) &&
 > !msg->msg_routing &&
 > lpni->lpni_ns_status == LNET_NI_STATUS_DOWN &&
 > lnet_peers_start_down())
 > return true;
 > 
 > This narrows it down so that non-router nodes are not affected.

What is the benefit of being so specific rather than checking only the msg_routing flag as I suggested in my last comment? Again, the only  messages that we want to drop are those that are being forwarded by a router, right?

Patch Set 2:

> > > (1 comment)
 > >
 > > If we come to this point in the function, it means our node is a
 > > router. If check_routers_before_use is enabled, we do want to
 > > consider the peer NI of the router to be alive if we are checking
 > > it - so we can ping it to proactively check on it.
 > > We can probably assume that the message our router initiates to
 > the
 > > other router is to check if it is alive. So how about the
 > > following:
 > >
 > > if (!list_empty(&lpni->lpni_peer_net->lpn_peer->lp_routes) &&
 > > !msg->msg_routing &&
 > > lpni->lpni_ns_status == LNET_NI_STATUS_DOWN &&
 > > lnet_peers_start_down())
 > > return true;
 > >
 > > This narrows it down so that non-router nodes are not affected.
 > 
 > What is the benefit of being so specific rather than checking only
 > the msg_routing flag as I suggested in my last comment? Again, the
 > only  messages that we want to drop are those that are being
 > forwarded by a router, right?
The difference is that checking only msg_routing flag allows messages to "down" non-router nis to go through as well. I thought that "check_routers_before_use" is only concerned with pinging routers, so in that case we do want to drop non-forwarded traffic to non-router peer nis is they are "down". If this can be neglected for whatever reason, then we can definitely go with just checking if the message is being routed.

Patch Set 2:

> The difference is that checking only msg_routing flag allows
 > messages to "down" non-router nis to go through as well. I thought
 > that "check_routers_before_use" is only concerned with pinging
 > routers, so in that case we do want to drop non-forwarded traffic
 > to non-router peer nis is they are "down". If this can be neglected
 > for whatever reason, then we can definitely go with just checking
 > if the message is being routed.

> in that case we do want to drop non-forwarded traffic to non-router peer nis is they are "down"

I am asserting that there is no reason to drop such messages. Can you think of a reason why we should be dropping this traffic?

Patch Set 2:

> > The difference is that checking only msg_routing flag allows
 > > messages to "down" non-router nis to go through as well. I
 > thought
 > > that "check_routers_before_use" is only concerned with pinging
 > > routers, so in that case we do want to drop non-forwarded traffic
 > > to non-router peer nis is they are "down". If this can be
 > neglected
 > > for whatever reason, then we can definitely go with just checking
 > > if the message is being routed.
 > 
 > > in that case we do want to drop non-forwarded traffic to
 > non-router peer nis is they are "down"
 > 
 > I am asserting that there is no reason to drop such messages. Can
 > you think of a reason why we should be dropping this traffic?

 > > The difference is that checking only msg_routing flag allows
 > > messages to "down" non-router nis to go through as well. I
 > thought
 > > that "check_routers_before_use" is only concerned with pinging
 > > routers, so in that case we do want to drop non-forwarded traffic
 > > to non-router peer nis is they are "down". If this can be
 > neglected
 > > for whatever reason, then we can definitely go with just checking
 > > if the message is being routed.
 > 
 > > in that case we do want to drop non-forwarded traffic to
 > non-router peer nis is they are "down"
 > 
 > I am asserting that there is no reason to drop such messages. Can
 > you think of a reason why we should be dropping this traffic?

I don't even know why there would be traffic initiated by a router to a non-router. The reason why my suggestion is more specific is to minimize the change in behaviour so it only addresses the issue with check_routers_before_use - between two router nodes, that is. 
Because currently we're already dropping non-routed traffic from router to a non-router ni in "down" state, the question should be what is gained by allowing such traffic.
I'm actually fine with going with your suggestion, just making my reasoning clear.

Patch Set 2:

For one thing it would be useful when troubleshooting issues between router and non-router.

If router considers some peer "down" and is dropping traffic to it, then we cannot currently log onto router and try to force traffic by sending a ping since router will just drop the ping before it gets to LND.

parents.subject: LU-16050 build: replace ofed_info with dpkg/rpm

commit.subject: LU-16106 lnet: allow direct messages regardless of peer NI status
commit.message:
LU-16106 lnet: allow direct messages regardless of peer NI status

If check_routers_before_use is enabled, the router needs to
be pinged before it is used, which is not possible because
its NIs are assumed to be down at start-up. Don't prevent
discovery of the router in this case.

This change allows non-routed traffic to peer NIs with "down"
status.

Test-Parameters: trivial
Signed-off-by: Serguei Smirnov <ssmirnov@whamcloud.com>
Change-Id: I36fa60e37ef4f47c82c69855c9b0b80bad8a36f4
Reviewed-on: https://review.whamcloud.com/48355
Reviewed-by: Frank Sehr <fsehr@whamcloud.com>
Reviewed-by: Chris Horn <chris.horn@hpe.com>
Tested-by: jenkins <devops@whamcloud.com>
Tested-by: Maloo <maloo@whamcloud.com>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>
